text:
The prospect of theParisOlympics is an exciting one but not one which holds any fear for me now I have a new mentality and mindset.

For so long, the Games felt a long way away, an overwhelming prospect with so many hurdles to clear beforehand. It didn’t seem like a reality for so long. But now we are almost there.

It was a huge wave of stress off my shoulders after the national championships in December and although I spoke to my coach, I have also learnt to deal with the pressure by myself.

I also didn’t want to spoil my experience or lessen the importance of other competitions by thinking about theOlympicstoo soon and that approach helped me centre myself.

It was always the end goal but a jam-packed schedule of nationals, followed by world championships and three World Cup legs stood in the way, plus a lot of travel, which can be the hardest part of being a professional athlete, but one I have learnt to live with and thrive on.

You get to visit some amazing countries, experience such cool cultures and eat great food, so we can’t complain! However, now I’m back in training with Paris as the end goal. I’m back on the ten-metre board, with a lot of support behind me.

The National Lottery supports us in so many different ways; one of which is providing us with the best support staff, including my strength and conditioning coach who helps me focus on strength-building and power training. This is vital for me going into the Games.

I speak to my team-mates about the prospect of the Olympics, of course, but your own experience is so valuable because it’s come from you. This cycle has been three years instead of four due to the 2020 Olympics taking place a year late because of the pandemic. I don’t know in all honestly if that has been a benefit or not.

Another year would have brought a lot of stress, I suppose, and my progress has been good since my Olympic debut in Tokyo and I want to get this over and done with now.

I have no expectations on myself. I want to enjoy it. Aged 16 and with internal battles being fought over my mental health, I didn’t enjoy myself in Tokyo in the slightest. I felt my Olympic experience was robbed from me because of the lack of joy I felt.

This time I have no school work to distract me or focus on in parallel and no excuses, and that helps me. I am in love with diving once again and I think any sportsperson would agree that when you love what you are doing, you train and therefore perform better.

I’m going into this Games completely changed. I was in such a dark place last time and thank god for helping me through.

It is another competition after all. I look back and can see drastic change on my face – I was sad, depressed and anxious at the last Olympics. I am a completely different person now, for the better.

Whatever happens in Paris, happens. It won’t make or break me. I know I have put my all into my training and preparation in the build-up to these Games and I am whole as a human being this time around. That is my mindset now and I’m excited.

title: I'm in love with my diving again and a Olympic winner whatever happens | Metro News
description: The prospect of the Paris Olympics is an exciting one but not one which holds any fear for me now I have a new mentality and mindset.
